title: Core Experiment Skeleton
purpose: Create one small, reusable experiment module that defines defaults and gives the project a single place to run an experiment later with different city counts.
why_simpler: Merges the old project skeleton and configuration work into one deliverable
Avoids building a large project structure too early
Lets the notebook and code grow around one clear entry point
scope: Choose one city-labeling convention
Define default fixed start city
Define brute-force city cap
Define the intended main experiment function shape
outputs: One small starter module or notebook code section with shared defaults
acceptance_criteria: Defaults are defined in one place
The project has a clear reusable entry point in mind
Nothing depends on a big package layout to get started

title: Distance Table Utilities
purpose: Implement the smallest complete set of table tools: representation, generation, loading, and selection by flag.
why_simpler: Merges the old data model, generator, and loader into one practical deliverable
Keeps all table logic together instead of spreading it across multiple milestones
Supports both generated and saved tables without extra architecture ceremony
scope: Define the table format
Generate a new asymmetric distance table
Load a saved table
Use a flag to choose generate versus load
Perform basic table validation
acceptance_criteria: A valid asymmetric table can be generated for city count n
A saved table can be loaded
The same function or workflow can choose between generated and saved input

title: Path Rules and Scoring
purpose: Create the shared path validation and scoring logic used by every algorithm.
why_simpler: Merges validation and scoring because they are naturally tested together
Reduces duplicate checks inside algorithm code
Creates one source of truth for correctness
scope: Validate that a path starts at the fixed start city
Validate that a path visits every city exactly once
Compute total directed path cost
acceptance_criteria: Known valid paths pass validation
Invalid paths fail validation
Known sample paths produce correct total costs

title: Three Baseline Algorithms
purpose: Implement guess-and-check, greedy, and brute force as the first comparison set.
why_simpler: Groups the three easiest-to-explain algorithms into one milestone
Gives a working comparison sooner
Lets brute force act as the truth check for small cases before annealing is added
scope: Implement guess-and-check from the fixed start city
Implement greedy from the fixed start city
Implement brute force with the city-count safety cap
acceptance_criteria: All three algorithms return valid paths
Brute force returns the best path on small known examples
Brute force skips cleanly above the configured cap

title: Annealing Algorithm
purpose: Add the fourth algorithm after the baseline comparison is already working.
why_simpler: Keeps annealing isolated because it is the most parameter-heavy piece
Prevents annealing complexity from blocking the simpler baseline milestone
Makes debugging much easier
scope: Build a valid annealing workflow starting from the fixed start city
Use neighbor changes that preserve path validity
Track current and best-so-far paths
acceptance_criteria: Returned annealing result is a valid Hamiltonian path
Best-so-far tracking works correctly
Algorithm can run on the same table as the baseline algorithms

title: Unified Experiment Runner
purpose: Wrap the table selection and all four algorithms into one reusable function and one consistent result format.
why_simpler: Merges result schema, comparison building, and main experiment orchestration into one deliverable
Avoids over-designing internal abstractions too early
Produces the thing the notebook actually needs: one call that returns usable results
scope: Run the selected table workflow
Run all four algorithms under shared rules
Normalize each result into one common structure
Build one comparison-ready summary
acceptance_criteria: One function call can run the full experiment
The function works with different city counts
The same workflow supports generated and saved tables
The summary clearly shows each algorithm result and any skipped brute-force run

title: Notebook Demo with Embedded Sanity Checks
purpose: Create one clean notebook that demonstrates the experiment and includes a few tiny correctness checks instead of a separate testing deliverable.
why_simpler: Merges notebook demonstration and correctness testing into one practical final step
Avoids a separate test framework deliverable during MVP buildout
Keeps the proof of correctness close to the classroom-facing artifact
scope: Explain the objective briefly
Show one generated or loaded table
Run the unified experiment function
Display the comparison output
Include a few small hand-checkable validation cells
acceptance_criteria: Notebook runs top to bottom
Notebook shows one complete experiment clearly
Notebook includes a few sanity checks for path validation, scoring, or tiny known cases
